title: Breast Surgery & Melanoma
subtitle: Surgical Oncology — Breast Conservation, Oncoplastics & Cutaneous Melanoma
guidelines_version: NCCN Breast Cancer v5.2025/v2.2026 · NCCN Melanoma v2.2025 · ESMO Breast 2024 · ESMO Melanoma 2023 · St Gallen Consensus 2023 · AJCC 8th Edition · SSO/ASTRO 2014
textbook_refs: Schwartz's Principles of Surgery, 11th ed., Ch. 17 & 16
Harris JR et al. Diseases of the Breast, 5th ed.
NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Breast Cancer v5.2025
NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Melanoma v2.2025
learning_objectives: Define the indications for breast-conserving surgery versus mastectomy
Apply the ACOSOG Z0011 criteria for sentinel node biopsy without axillary dissection
Describe the surgical steps of wire/seed-guided wide local excision
Interpret molecular subtypes (Luminal A/B, HER2+, TNBC) and their surgical implications
Outline the indications and techniques for oncoplastic breast surgery
Select candidates for neoadjuvant chemotherapy and plan post-NAC surgical management
Choose between implant-based and autologous immediate breast reconstruction
Stage cutaneous melanoma using the AJCC 8th Edition T/N/M classification
Prescribe evidence-based wide excision margins according to Breslow thickness (NCCN 2025)
Apply MSLT-I and MSLT-II findings to sentinel node biopsy and completion dissection decisions
Select systemic therapy for Stage III/IV melanoma based on BRAF mutation status

blocks.content: Breast-conserving surgery (BCS) — wide local excision (WLE) with clear margins followed by whole-breast radiotherapy — is oncologically equivalent to mastectomy for most stage I-III breast cancers. This equivalence was established by the landmark Milan Trial (Veronesi, NEJM 2002) and NSABP B-06 (Fisher, NEJM 2002) with up to 20-year follow-up. BCS should be offered to all women without absolute contraindications (inability to receive RT, multicentric disease requiring non-achievable margins, patient preference for mastectomy).

blocks.title: Contraindications to Breast-Conserving Surgery
rows: May be overcome by oncoplastic techniques in selected cases
Prior breast irradiation to the same breast
Connective tissue disease increases RT toxicity
RT deferred; mastectomy may be preferred to avoid RT exposure
Equivalent local recurrence; contralateral risk counselling mandatory

blocks.title: ACOSOG Z0011 (Giuliano, JAMA 2011/2017)
blocks.content: **Population:** 891 women with T1-T2 breast cancer, 1–2 positive sentinel nodes, BCS + whole-breast RT. **Result:** 10-year OS 86.3% SLN alone vs 83.6% ALND (non-inferiority p=0.02); locoregional recurrence equivalent. **Practice change:** Completion axillary lymph node dissection eliminated for this patient group. Patient eligibility: T1-T2, 1-2 positive SLN, BCS planned, whole-breast RT planned, no neoadjuvant therapy, clinically node-negative.

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Margin Width + NCCN 2026: SLNB Omission
blocks.content: **Margin width:** For invasive breast cancer with BCS, the SSO/ASTRO consensus (2014) defines adequate margin as **no ink on tumour** — a 1mm or 2mm margin is not required. For DCIS, a 2mm margin is recommended. **NCCN v2.2026 — SLNB Omission:** SLNB may be omitted for carefully selected patients with **HR+/HER2−, cT1, cN0** undergoing BCS with planned whole-breast RT and endocrine therapy. Based on SOUND (Lancet Oncology 2023), INSEMA, and BOOG 2013-08 trials showing axillary recurrence rates of only 0.4–0.9% at 5 years. Criteria: postmenopausal >50 years, HR+/HER2−, grade 1–2, cT1N0, agreeable to whole-breast RT + endocrine therapy. Does NOT apply to HER2+ or TNBC subtypes or post-NAC patients.

Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) is the standard axillary staging procedure for clinically node-negative (cN0) breast cancer. Dual-tracer technique (radioisotope 99mTc-labelled colloid + blue dye or fluorescent tracer) achieves the highest detection rate (>96%) with lowest false-negative rate (<5%). The procedure: peritumoral or periareolar injection of tracer; intraoperative gamma probe guidance to identify the hot node(s); visual identification of blue-stained nodes; ex vivo counting. Minimum 3 sentinel nodes should be retrieved; all hot and/or blue nodes are removed.

blocks.title: Common Pitfall — Z0011 Eligibility
blocks.content: Do not omit ALND outside Z0011 criteria. Z0011 does NOT apply to: mastectomy patients; patients with ≥3 positive SLN; patients who received neoadjuvant chemotherapy; patients not planned for whole-breast RT. Applying Z0011 findings outside these criteria risks inadequate axillary management.

blocks.title: Breast Cancer Molecular Subtypes — Surgical Implications
rows: BCS preferred; Oncotype DX may guide adjuvant chemo
ER+, HER2-, high Ki-67 OR HER2+
Yes — BRCAm: olaparib; high pCR rate

blocks.title: Board Pearl — BRCA1/2 and Surgical Decision
blocks.content: **BRCA1/2 mutations** do not change the indication for BCS vs mastectomy from an oncological standpoint — local recurrence rates are equivalent. However, mutation carriers have a significantly elevated contralateral breast cancer risk (BRCA1: 65%, BRCA2: 45% lifetime) and bilateral risk-reducing mastectomy should be discussed. The surgical decision is driven by patient preference and contralateral risk management, not ipsilateral recurrence risk.

Neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NAC) is administered before surgery to downstage the primary tumour and axilla, enabling breast-conserving surgery in patients who would otherwise require mastectomy, and to assess tumour chemosensitivity in vivo. In addition, pathological complete response (pCR — no residual invasive cancer in breast or nodes, ypT0/is ypN0) is a validated surrogate for improved long-term survival in HER2+ and TNBC subtypes. Subtypes most likely to benefit from and respond to NAC are: HER2+ (dual HER2 blockade with pertuzumab/trastuzumab + chemotherapy; pCR 40–65%), TNBC (platinum-containing regimens; pCR 35–55%), and large Luminal B (high Ki-67; pCR lower but downstaging meaningful). Luminal A tumours have very low pCR rates and rarely benefit from NAC — neoadjuvant endocrine therapy is an option for elderly/frail patients.

blocks.title: Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy — Subtypes, Regimens, and Residual Disease Management
rows: T-DM1 for residual; consider CDK4/6 inhibitors for HR+ residual
Capecitabine × 8 cycles (CREATE-X trial); olaparib if BRCAm
Adjuvant CDK4/6 inhibitor (abemaciclib MONARCH-E); consider extended endocrine therapy

blocks.title: Board Pearl — pCR and Survival Surrogate
blocks.content: pCR (ypT0/is ypN0) predicts improved event-free survival and OS in **HER2+ and TNBC** but is a weaker surrogate in HR+ (Luminal) subtypes. The FDA has accepted pCR as a regulatory endpoint for accelerated approval in these high-proliferative subtypes. For Luminal tumours, residual cancer burden (RCB score) provides better prognostic discrimination than pCR alone.

Oncoplastic breast surgery (OPS) combines oncological resection with immediate plastic surgical reconstruction techniques to achieve tumour-free margins while preserving acceptable breast shape and volume. OPS has expanded the indications for breast-conserving surgery, enabling resection of larger tumours and challenging locations (central, lower pole, medial) that would otherwise require mastectomy. Two broad categories of oncoplastic techniques exist: volume displacement (rearranging existing breast tissue) and volume replacement (importing tissue from outside the breast).

blocks.title: Oncoplastic Breast Surgery — Technique Classification and Indications
rows: Glandular advancement flaps, batwing/hatchet excision, round block (periareolar)
Therapeutic reduction mammoplasty (inverted-T, vertical scar), wise pattern, inferior pedicle
Large-breasted women; central/lower tumours; ptotic breasts
Thoracodorsal artery perforator (TAP) flap, lateral intercostal perforator (LICAP) flap
Upper outer/inner quadrant; partial mastectomy defect

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Tumour:Breast Ratio >20%
blocks.content: A **tumour:breast ratio >20%** is the accepted threshold at which standard WLE is likely to produce an unacceptable cosmetic deformity. At this ratio, oncoplastic level II techniques (reduction mammoplasty, large volume displacement) or volume replacement should be considered. In large-breasted women with macromastia, therapeutic mammoplasty (resection of tumour combined with reduction) provides simultaneous oncological and aesthetic benefit and may offer the opportunity to irradiate a smaller breast volume.

sections.title: Immediate Breast Reconstruction — Implant-Based vs Autologous

blocks.content: Immediate breast reconstruction (IBR) at the time of mastectomy offers psychological, oncological, and practical advantages over delayed reconstruction. IBR does not delay adjuvant chemotherapy (typically starting 3–4 weeks post-mastectomy) or radiation (6–8 weeks post-surgery). Two broad approaches exist: implant-based reconstruction (IBR) — the most common technique worldwide — and autologous tissue reconstruction, which uses the patient's own tissue to recreate the breast mound.

blocks.title: Immediate Breast Reconstruction — Implant-Based vs Autologous Comparison
rows: High: capsular contracture, implant failure (30–50% with RT)
Moderate: fat necrosis but autologous tissue tolerates RT better
Implant exchange may be needed; 10yr revision ~30%

blocks.title: Common Pitfall — Radiation Effect on Reconstruction
blocks.content: Post-mastectomy radiotherapy (PMRT) significantly increases the risk of implant-based reconstruction failure (capsular contracture, implant rupture, chronic pain, poor cosmesis): incidence 30–50% with PMRT vs 5–15% without. When PMRT is anticipated (4+ positive nodes, T3/T4, close margins), discuss with the patient the option of: (1) delayed reconstruction after radiation; (2) autologous reconstruction (DIEP/TRAM tolerate radiation better); or (3) two-stage TE with planned conversion to autologous tissue after radiation. The tissue expander should remain deflated during radiation and exchanged to permanent implant or autologous reconstruction 3–6 months post-RT.

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Nipple-Sparing Mastectomy Criteria
blocks.content: Nipple-sparing mastectomy (NSM) is oncologically safe when: tumour is >2 cm from the nipple; nipple-areola complex (NAC) is not involved clinically or on MRI; no bloody nipple discharge; no Paget's disease; nipple not inverted due to tumour. Intraoperative frozen section of the retroareolar tissue is mandatory — if positive, the NAC must be excised. NSM is contraindicated in inflammatory breast cancer and when subareolar tumour extension is confirmed.

Axillary management after NAC is complex because standard SLNB in a previously node-positive (cN+) patient has a higher false-negative rate (FNR) than in cN0 patients. Three landmark prospective studies — FALSE trial (Netherlands), ACOSOG Z1071 (US), and SENTINA (Germany) — assessed the accuracy of post-NAC SLNB in initially node-positive patients who converted to ypN0. All three demonstrated FNR of 12–14% with dual-tracer SLNB alone, which exceeds the acceptable threshold of <10%. This FNR can be reduced to <5% by targeted axillary dissection (TAD).

Targeted Axillary Dissection (TAD) is the recommended strategy for ypN0 assessment after NAC in initially node-positive patients. TAD consists of: (1) clip placement in the biopsy-proven positive node at diagnosis; (2) post-NAC seed/wire localisation of the clipped node; (3) surgical excision of the sentinel nodes PLUS the clipped (marked) node, regardless of whether it is a sentinel node. Retrieval of the marked node is confirmed by specimen radiography. Studies show TAD reduces FNR to 2–4%, making axillary dissection avoidable in patients achieving ypN0 with TAD.

blocks.title: Board Pearl — ypN0 After NAC: Is ALND Still Needed?
blocks.content: **ypN0 with TAD (clipped node retrieved + ≥3 SLN removed):** ALND can be safely omitted. Multiple studies demonstrate that patients achieving ypN0 with adequate TAD have similar locoregional recurrence rates with axillary irradiation (regional nodal irradiation) substituting for ALND. **ypN1 (residual nodal disease):** ALND is still required in most cases, as residual axillary disease has significant implications for locoregional control and systemic therapy intensification.

sections.title: Cutaneous Melanoma — Pathology, Classification & AJCC 8th Staging

blocks.content: Cutaneous melanoma arises from malignant transformation of melanocytes. Incidence continues to rise — it accounts for only 4% of skin cancers but 80% of skin cancer deaths. Key clinicopathological subtypes include: **superficial spreading melanoma** (SSM, most common, 70%, radial growth phase before vertical invasion); **nodular melanoma** (NM, 15–30%, de novo vertical growth, poor prognosis); **lentigo maligna melanoma** (LMM, 5–10%, chronically sun-exposed skin, often facial, elderly patients, broad radial growth); **acral lentiginous melanoma** (ALM, 5%, palms/soles/subungual, overrepresented in non-white populations, no UV causation); and **desmoplastic melanoma** (perineural invasion, high local recurrence, low sentinel node positivity rate).

The AJCC 8th Edition (2017) staging system for cutaneous melanoma is based on: **T category** (Breslow thickness + ulceration, ± mitotic rate for T1); **N category** (nodal burden: micrometastases detected by SLNB vs macrometastases detected clinically; in-transit/satellite/microsatellite disease); and **M category** (site of distant metastasis and LDH elevation). The **Breslow thickness** — measured from the granular layer of the epidermis to the deepest tumour cell — remains the single most important independent prognostic factor. The **Clark level** (anatomical depth) has been eliminated from the 8th edition staging for T2-T4 melanoma.

blocks.title: AJCC 8th Edition — T Category Melanoma Staging
rows: Lowest risk group; SLNB generally not indicated
< 0.8 mm with ulceration OR 0.8–1.0 mm ± ulceration
SLNB recommended; worse prognosis than T2a
High risk; adjuvant systemic therapy indicated if Stage III
Highest T-category risk; adjuvant therapy mandatory if operable Stage III

blocks.title: AJCC 8th Edition — N and M Categories
rows: No regional nodes, but in-transit/satellite/microsatellite lesion(s)
4+ nodes, matted, or any in-transit + ≥2 nodes
Skin, subcutaneous, or distant lymph node mets
Worst prognosis; targeted therapy + stereotactic RT

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Ulceration and Microsatellites
blocks.content: **Ulceration** upstages T category (T1a→T1b, T2a→T2b, etc.) and is defined as absence of an intact epidermis overlying the melanoma on histology — not macroscopic ulceration. It represents an adverse biological feature independent of Breslow thickness. **Microsatellite lesions** (discontinuous tumour nests >0.05mm separated from the main tumour mass by normal dermis, detected on primary excision specimen) are now classified as N1c/N2c/N3 in AJCC 8th and indicate SLNB followed by systemic therapy consideration, even without nodal disease.

blocks.title: Key Prognostic Factors — AJCC 8th Melanoma
blocks.content: In order of prognostic importance: **(1) Breslow thickness** — most important factor for localised disease; **(2) Ulceration** — independent poor prognostic factor at every T stage; **(3) Sentinel lymph node status** — most important for Stage II vs III distinction; **(4) Number of positive nodes** — N1 vs N2 vs N3; **(5) Mitotic rate** — retained for T1 substaging (T1b includes any mitotic rate in some classifications); **(6) LDH** — elevated LDH in Stage IV is M1c/1d modifier; **(7) BRAF V600 mutation status** — predicts systemic therapy selection, not stage.

sections.title: Wide Excision Margins in Melanoma — Evidence & Technique

blocks.content: Wide local excision (WLE) of cutaneous melanoma is performed after initial diagnostic excision biopsy (which should ideally be a narrow-margin complete excisional biopsy of the primary lesion, not punch or shave biopsy, to allow accurate Breslow measurement). The definitive wide excision is performed with specific margins determined by Breslow thickness — these margins represent the result of four landmark randomised controlled trials (WHO Melanoma Trial 1, MSLT-I margin component, UK Melanoma Study Group, Swedish Melanoma Study Group, and Intergroup Melanoma Surgical Trial) that collectively established the current evidence-based margin recommendations.

blocks.title: NCCN 2025 — Recommended Wide Excision Margins by Breslow Thickness
rows: Expert consensus; staged excision (Mohs) for facial/LMM
Larger margins or staged surgery for lentigo maligna on face
WHO Trial 1; Balch JAMA 2001; UK MGS
1 cm equivalent to wider margins for thin melanoma
1 cm statistically acceptable; 2 cm preferred when anatomically feasible
Intergroup Melanoma Surgical Trial; Gillgren LANCET 2011
3 cm NOT superior to 2 cm in randomised trials
Expert consensus based on trial extrapolation
3 cm not proven superior; anatomical constraints often limit to 2 cm

blocks.content: The **deep margin** of wide excision should include the deep fascia (fascia of the underlying muscle) but need not remove muscle unless the tumour directly overlies or invades fascial structures. Fascial excision does not improve survival but reduces local recurrence risk at the primary site. For **subungual melanoma**, amputation at the distal interphalangeal joint (DIP) provides adequate margins for digit preservation while achieving local control. For **facial melanoma** or anatomically constrained sites, the desired 1–2 cm margin may not be achievable — staged excision, Mohs micrographic surgery with melanoma-specific staining, or reduced margins with RT may be necessary, but this should be discussed in a multidisciplinary setting.

blocks.title: Board Pearl — The 3 cm Margin Is Not Superior
blocks.content: The **MelMarT (Gillgren, Lancet 2011)** and UK Melanoma Study Group trials definitively showed that **3 cm excision margins are NOT superior to 2 cm** for T3-T4 melanoma in terms of locoregional recurrence, distant recurrence, or overall survival. The 2 cm margin is now the standard maximum recommended margin for melanomas >2 mm Breslow thickness. Wider margins only increase surgical morbidity (need for flaps/grafts) without oncological benefit.

blocks.title: Common Pitfall — Initial Biopsy Technique
blocks.content: Punch biopsy or shave biopsy should NOT be used for suspected melanoma — these techniques may transect the lesion, preventing accurate Breslow thickness measurement and understaging the tumour. The preferred initial biopsy is **narrow-margin excisional biopsy** (1–2 mm margins) of the entire lesion. For large lesions or functionally difficult locations (face, acral), a **full-thickness incisional punch** of the most representative/thickest area is acceptable but suboptimal. Do NOT perform a wide local excision as the initial biopsy — this prevents subsequent SLNB mapping.

sections.title: Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy in Melanoma — MSLT-I & MSLT-II

blocks.content: Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) in melanoma provides accurate pathological staging, identifies patients with occult nodal disease who may benefit from regional therapy and systemic adjuvant treatment, and stratifies prognosis. The technique uses intradermal injection of technetium-99m-labelled colloid (and/or blue dye/ICG fluorescence) around the primary melanoma site (not scar) to identify the sentinel node(s) via lymphoscintigraphy and intraoperative gamma probe. Melanoma SLNB should be performed at the time of wide excision — not as a staged procedure if avoidable.

blocks.title: MSLT-I (Morton, NEJM 2006 / Lancet Oncol 2014)
blocks.content: **Population:** 1347 patients with intermediate-thickness (1.2–3.5 mm) or thick (>3.5 mm) melanoma randomised to wide excision + SLNB (with immediate completion CLND if positive) vs wide excision alone (CLND only at clinical nodal relapse). **Results:** 10-year melanoma-specific survival: no significant difference in the overall groups, but among patients with nodal disease, those identified by SLNB had **significantly better 10-year survival** than those identified at clinical relapse (62.1% vs 41.5%). **Practice change:** SLNB provides accurate staging and **early CLND in SLNB+ patients** improves melanoma-specific survival vs delayed CLND at relapse, for intermediate-thickness tumours.

blocks.title: MSLT-II (Faries, NEJM 2017)
blocks.content: **Population:** 1934 patients with positive SLNB randomised to immediate completion lymph node dissection (CLND) vs ultrasound surveillance of the nodal basin. **Results:** Melanoma-specific survival at 3 years: 86% CLND vs 86% surveillance (non-significant difference). **CLND improved:** disease-free survival (68% vs 63%) and regional nodal control. **CLND increased:** lymphedema (24% vs 6%). **Practice change:** CLND after positive SLNB does NOT improve melanoma-specific survival. Ultrasound surveillance of the regional basin is an acceptable and now preferred alternative to CLND in patients with micrometastatic sentinel node disease.

blocks.title: SLNB Indications in Melanoma — NCCN 2025
rows: SLN positivity rate 35–40%; adjuvant therapy if positive
SLN positivity rate <5% even with thick tumours; exception to standard algorithm

blocks.title: Board Pearl — What to Do After a Positive SLNB (MSLT-II Era)
blocks.content: After a positive SLNB in 2025, the recommendation is: **do NOT perform routine completion CLND** — MSLT-II demonstrated no survival benefit and significant lymphedema risk (24%). Instead: (1) **Ultrasound surveillance** of the regional nodal basin every 3–4 months for 2 years; (2) **Adjuvant systemic therapy**: nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib+trametinib (if BRAF+) for Stage III — reduces distant recurrence. CLND is still indicated for: macroscopic extracapsular extension; clinically palpable nodes (Stage IIIB/C); or patient/surgeon preference after counselling. The shift is from 'CLND is standard' to 'surveillance is standard' for occult micrometastatic sentinel node disease.

sections.title: Systemic Therapy for Advanced Melanoma — Immunotherapy & Targeted Therapy

blocks.content: The treatment of advanced (Stage III/IV) melanoma has been transformed since 2011 by two therapeutic revolutions: **checkpoint immunotherapy** (anti-CTLA-4 and anti-PD-1/PD-L1 antibodies) and **BRAF-targeted therapy** (BRAF and MEK inhibitors for BRAF V600-mutated melanoma). Approximately **50% of cutaneous melanomas** harbour a BRAF V600 mutation (predominantly V600E, ~80%; V600K, ~15%). BRAF mutation testing is mandatory in all Stage III/IV melanoma before systemic therapy selection. Mucosal and uveal melanomas have significantly lower BRAF mutation rates (<5%) and different molecular landscapes.

blocks.title: Systemic Therapy for Advanced Melanoma — Key Regimens and Trials
rows: First-line standard regardless of BRAF status; 59% grade 3/4 toxicity
Alternative to combo; lower toxicity than nivo+ipi
BRAF+ only; rapid response; resistance develops in ~50% by 1 yr
Better tolerability profile vs older BRAF+MEK combos

blocks.title: CheckMate 067 — 7-Year Follow-Up (Wolchok, NEJM 2022)
blocks.content: **Population:** 945 patients with advanced (unresectable Stage III/IV) melanoma randomised to nivolumab + ipilimumab vs nivolumab alone vs ipilimumab alone. **7-year results:** OS: 49% (nivo+ipi) vs 44% (nivo) vs 21% (ipi). Median OS: 72.1 months (nivo+ipi) vs 36.9 months (nivo) vs 19.9 months (ipi). **PD-L1 status:** In PD-L1 ≥1% tumours, nivo+ipi and nivo monotherapy had similar OS. In PD-L1 <1%, nivo+ipi was superior to nivo alone. **Practice change:** Nivolumab+ipilimumab is the preferred first-line standard for most advanced melanoma patients; nivolumab monotherapy acceptable for PD-L1 high or patients unsuitable for combination toxicity.

blocks.title: Adjuvant Systemic Therapy for Resected Stage III Melanoma
rows: Superior to adjuvant ipilimumab; 12 months duration
BRAF+ only; 12 months; OS benefit confirmed at 5yr (55% vs 47%)
Higher toxicity (grade 3/4: 41%); largely replaced by anti-PD-1 agents

blocks.title: Board Pearl — BRAF+ Advanced Melanoma: Immunotherapy or Targeted Therapy First?
blocks.content: In BRAF V600-mutated advanced melanoma, both immunotherapy (nivo+ipi) and targeted therapy (dabrafenib+trametinib) achieve ~50% 5-year OS. **Current practice (2025):** No prospective head-to-head trial definitively establishes superiority of one approach as first-line. **Arguments for immunotherapy first:** durable responses (plateau in survival curves); responses to targeted therapy after PD-1 failure are maintained. **Arguments for targeted therapy first:** rapid cytoreduction in high-volume/symptomatic disease; bridging to immunotherapy. Most melanoma centres (ESMO 2023) prefer **first-line immunotherapy** (nivo+ipi) for most BRAF+ patients without urgent need for rapid response, reserving targeted therapy for second line or for patients with high-volume disease requiring rapid control. Sequencing remains an area of active investigation (DREAMseq trial, SECOMBIT trial).

blocks.title: In-Transit Melanoma — Regional Therapy Options
blocks.content: **In-transit metastases** (dermal/subdermal lesions between primary site and regional lymph nodes, more than 2 cm from primary) indicate locoregional disease progression. Management options: **(1) Surgery:** resection if limited/resectable disease. **(2) Intralesional T-VEC** (talimogene laherparepvec — oncolytic HSV-1 virus): approved for unresectable cutaneous/subcutaneous/nodal melanoma; durable response rate 16%; excellent tolerability. **(3) Isolated limb perfusion (ILP)** or **isolated limb infusion (ILI):** hyperthermic melphalan ± TNF-α delivered to the isolated limb via extracorporeal circuit; overall response rate 60–80%; complete response 40–50%; standard for extensive/unresectable in-transit disease of extremities. **(4) Systemic immunotherapy** (pembrolizumab/nivolumab ± ipilimumab): preferred when systemic disease concurrent or systemic therapy appropriate.

consolidation.intro: Test your knowledge of breast surgery and melanoma. 25 questions covering all major topics.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman has a 2.2cm T2 N0 M0 ER+/PR+/HER2- breast cancer in the upper outer quadrant. She strongly prefers breast conservation. She has no prior breast irradiation and no connective tissue disease. What is the most appropriate surgical approach?

options.A: Mastectomy — tumour >2cm is a contraindication to BCS
options.B: Wide local excision (BCS) + sentinel node biopsy + adjuvant whole-breast radiotherapy
options.C: BCS without sentinel node biopsy — lymph node staging is not required for T2 tumours
options.D: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy to reduce tumour size before BCS

explanation.correct: BCS is appropriate for T2 N0 tumours when R0 resection with acceptable cosmesis is achievable. Tumour size alone does not contraindicate BCS. SLNB is mandatory for all invasive breast cancers undergoing surgery. Whole-breast radiotherapy after BCS is required for local control.

wrong.A: T2 (>2cm but ≤5cm) is not a contraindication to BCS. The indication is based on achieving clear margins and acceptable cosmesis.
wrong.C: SLNB is required for all invasive breast cancers undergoing surgery for staging — regardless of clinical N0 status.
wrong.D: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy is not indicated for Luminal A tumours — these respond poorly to chemotherapy.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Breast Cancer v5.2025 — BCS appropriate for stage I-II tumours with achievable negative margins; SLNB for all invasive cancers
explanation.trial: Milan Trial (Veronesi, NEJM 2002): quadrantectomy vs mastectomy — equivalent 20-year OS. NSABP B-06: lumpectomy + RT equivalent to mastectomy.
explanation.pearl: Always discuss reconstruction options at the time of surgical planning — even for BCS. Oncoplastic techniques can achieve excellent cosmesis for larger excisions.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman with a 3 cm ER-/PR-/HER2- (TNBC) breast cancer receives neoadjuvant AC-paclitaxel. Pathological assessment after surgery reveals residual invasive carcinoma in the breast (ypT1b) and one positive lymph node (ypN1). What is the most appropriate adjuvant systemic therapy?

options.A: No further systemic therapy — chemotherapy was already given pre-operatively
options.D: Adjuvant olaparib — all TNBC patients with residual disease should receive PARP inhibitors

explanation.correct: The CREATE-X trial (Masuda et al., NEJM 2017) demonstrated that adjuvant capecitabine for 8 cycles in HER2-negative breast cancer patients with residual disease after NAC significantly improved disease-free survival and OS (particularly in TNBC: 5-year OS 78.8% vs 70.3%). Capecitabine is now standard adjuvant therapy for TNBC and HR+ HER2- patients with residual disease after NAC.

wrong.A: Residual disease after NAC is a high-risk feature requiring treatment intensification — not observation.
wrong.C: T-DM1 is the standard for HER2+ residual disease (KATHERINE trial), not TNBC. This patient is HER2-.
wrong.D: Olaparib (PARP inhibitor) is indicated for germline BRCA1/2-mutated HER2-negative breast cancer in the adjuvant setting (OlympiA trial) — only in BRCA mutation carriers, not all TNBC patients.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Breast v5.2025 — adjuvant capecitabine for non-pCR after NAC in HER2- breast cancer
explanation.trial: CREATE-X (Masuda, NEJM 2017): n=910 HER2- non-pCR patients; capecitabine improved 5yr DFS (74.1% vs 67.6%) and OS; TNBC subgroup had largest benefit.
explanation.pearl: Residual disease algorithm after NAC: HER2+ residual → T-DM1 (KATHERINE); TNBC residual → capecitabine (CREATE-X) ± olaparib if BRCA+; HR+ HER2- residual → capecitabine ± CDK4/6 inhibitor (MONARCH-E).

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with a 5 cm erythematous right breast with peau d'orange skin changes involving more than one-third of the breast. The nipple is retracted. Core biopsy shows ER+/HER2- invasive ductal carcinoma. The most appropriate initial diagnosis and management is:

options.A: T3 locally advanced breast cancer — start neoadjuvant chemotherapy
options.B: Inflammatory breast cancer (IBC) — start neoadjuvant chemotherapy; mastectomy is the eventual surgical treatment
options.C: Locally recurrent breast cancer — re-excision of the primary site
options.D: T4b breast cancer — upfront mastectomy followed by adjuvant chemotherapy

explanation.correct: Inflammatory breast cancer (IBC) is a clinical diagnosis requiring: (1) rapid onset of erythema, edema (peau d'orange), and/or skin warmth involving ≥1/3 of the breast; (2) pathological confirmation of carcinoma. IBC is classified as T4d and is treated with neoadjuvant chemotherapy first followed by modified radical mastectomy. Post-mastectomy radiotherapy is mandatory for all IBC.

wrong.A: T3 locally advanced breast cancer is a large tumour (>5 cm) without skin involvement — skin changes and peau d'orange define a different category.
wrong.C: There is no indication of prior breast surgery. This is de novo IBC.
wrong.D: T4b (ulceration or skin nodules) does not capture the clinical picture of IBC (which is T4d). Upfront mastectomy is contraindicated in IBC — NAC must precede surgery.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Breast v5.2025 — IBC: T4d; NAC mandatory first; modified radical mastectomy + PMRT
explanation.trial: Cristofanilli M et al., JCO 2007: IBC clinical diagnostic criteria established; neoadjuvant strategy associated with improved outcomes vs upfront surgery.
explanation.pearl: IBC diagnosis = clinical (erythema + edema ≥1/3 breast + rapid onset) + pathological (biopsy confirming carcinoma). BCS is absolutely contraindicated in IBC. PMRT is mandatory regardless of nodal status or surgical margins.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman undergoes modified radical mastectomy. Final pathology shows 5 positive axillary nodes, ER+/HER2-, T2 primary tumour. What is the indication for post-mastectomy radiotherapy (PMRT)?

options.A: PMRT is not indicated — ER+ tumours rarely recur locoregionally
options.B: PMRT is indicated — ≥4 positive axillary nodes is a standard indication for PMRT
options.C: PMRT is only indicated if margins are positive — 5 nodes without margin involvement does not meet criteria
options.D: PMRT is indicated only for HER2+ or TNBC subtypes after mastectomy

explanation.correct: The standard indications for PMRT are: (1) ≥4 positive axillary nodes; (2) T3/T4 primary tumour; (3) positive/close surgical margins. Five positive nodes clearly meets indication (1). The EBCTCG meta-analysis (Lancet 2014) demonstrated that PMRT reduces 10-year locoregional recurrence by ~10% and improves 20-year breast cancer-specific survival by ~4% in women with 1–3 or ≥4 positive nodes. PMRT recommendations are independent of molecular subtype.

wrong.A: Molecular subtype does not negate the indication for PMRT. ER+ tumours with ≥4 positive nodes have significant locoregional recurrence risk without PMRT.
wrong.C: PMRT indications include nodal involvement (≥4 nodes) independently of margin status.
wrong.D: PMRT indications apply to all breast cancer subtypes — molecular subtype does not determine PMRT eligibility.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Breast v5.2025 — PMRT: ≥4 positive nodes; T3/T4; positive margins; consider 1–3 nodes (multidisciplinary)
explanation.trial: EBCTCG meta-analysis (Clarke, Lancet 2014): PMRT reduced 10-yr recurrence ~10% and improved 20-yr survival in node-positive disease.
explanation.pearl: PMRT indications: ≥4+ nodes (definitive); T3/T4 primary (definitive); positive margins (definitive). 1–3 nodes: individualised decision (PMRT decreases locoregional recurrence but OS benefit smaller in 1–3 node category).

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman requires mastectomy for a 6 cm TNBC. She has adequate abdominal tissue. Post-mastectomy radiotherapy (PMRT) is anticipated given 6 positive axillary nodes. Which reconstruction strategy best minimises long-term complications from the combination of mastectomy + PMRT?

options.B: Immediate tissue expander followed by exchange to permanent implant after PMRT
options.C: Delayed autologous DIEP flap reconstruction after completion of PMRT
options.D: No reconstruction — PMRT makes reconstruction unsafe

explanation.correct: When PMRT is anticipated, immediate implant-based reconstruction carries a 30–50% failure rate (capsular contracture, implant rupture, aesthetic failure). The optimal strategy when PMRT is planned is: delayed autologous reconstruction (DIEP flap) after completing all adjuvant therapy including PMRT. Autologous tissue tolerates prior radiation better than implants, achieving superior cosmetic outcomes. The DIEP flap is performed 6–12 months after completion of RT when tissue healing is complete.

wrong.A: Direct-to-implant reconstruction has the highest failure rate with PMRT — not appropriate when PMRT is planned.
wrong.B: A tissue expander can serve as a temporary placeholder during RT (deflated during radiation) to preserve the skin envelope, but it should be exchanged to an autologous reconstruction (not permanent implant) after RT for best outcomes.
wrong.D: PMRT does not preclude reconstruction — delayed autologous reconstruction is specifically designed for this scenario and is highly successful.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Breast v5.2025 / ASTRO Guidelines — PMRT planned: consider delayed reconstruction; autologous preferred over implant post-radiation
explanation.trial: Albornoz CR et al., Ann Surg Oncol 2015: systematic review — implant-based reconstruction with PMRT: 30–50% complication rate; autologous after PMRT: superior outcomes.
explanation.pearl: Algorithm: PMRT anticipated → (1) delayed reconstruction after RT (best option) OR (2) TE as placeholder during RT then autologous reconstruction. Avoid implant as final reconstruction if PMRT planned. DIEP = gold standard autologous reconstruction.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman presents with unilateral nipple eczema that has persisted for 8 weeks despite topical steroids. Breast examination is unremarkable. Mammogram is normal. What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.A: MRI breast — Paget's disease is only visible on MRI
options.B: Punch biopsy or scrape cytology of the nipple skin — to diagnose Paget's disease
options.C: Continue topical steroids for 4 more weeks — 12 weeks total is required before biopsy
options.D: Refer to dermatology — nipple eczema not responding to steroids is a dermatological emergency

explanation.correct: Persistent unilateral nipple changes (eczema, ulceration, crusting, erythema) not responding to topical treatment should be biopsied to exclude Paget's disease of the nipple. Diagnosis requires punch biopsy or nipple scrape cytology demonstrating the pathognomonic Paget cells (large clear cells with prominent nucleoli) within the epidermis. Mammogram may be normal — the underlying carcinoma may not be detectable on mammogram alone; MRI is the preferred adjunct imaging.

wrong.A: MRI is a useful adjunct to stage the extent of Paget's disease but cannot provide the histological diagnosis — biopsy is required first.
wrong.C: Eight weeks of failed topical steroids is sufficient to mandate biopsy. Persisting without histological diagnosis risks delayed treatment of an underlying malignancy.
wrong.D: Dermatology referral may be appropriate for bilateral nipple eczema, but unilateral non-responding eczema in a woman >40 requires breast surgery evaluation and biopsy first.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Breast v5.2025 — unilateral nipple eczema not responding to steroids: biopsy to exclude Paget's disease
explanation.trial: Kothari AS et al., Eur J Surg Oncol 2002: Paget's disease — nipple biopsy diagnostic; associated with underlying DCIS or invasive cancer in 80–100% of cases.
explanation.pearl: Paget's disease of the nipple: unilateral > bilateral; associated with underlying DCIS/invasive cancer; biopsy = Paget cells (clear cells in epidermis); treatment: central lumpectomy + RT or mastectomy. Bilateral nipple eczema → dermatitis; unilateral → Paget's until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman has a 4 cm ER+/PR+/HER2- breast cancer with 2 axillary nodes positive on core biopsy. She undergoes BCS after neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Post-NAC pathology shows ypT0 ypN0 (complete pathological response). When should sentinel node biopsy be performed in this setting?

options.A: SLNB should be performed before NAC to accurately stage the axilla
options.B: SLNB should not be performed in cN+ patients treated with NAC — ALND is always required
options.C: Post-NAC SLNB with targeted axillary dissection (TAD) is appropriate; ypN0 with adequate sampling allows ALND omission
options.D: SLNB is not needed if pCR is achieved in the breast — axillary status can be assumed negative

explanation.correct: In cN+ patients undergoing NAC, the appropriate axillary management strategy is post-NAC SLNB with TAD (targeted axillary dissection — retrieval of both sentinel nodes and the pre-treatment clipped node). If ypN0 is confirmed with adequate sampling (≥3 sentinel nodes retrieved AND clipped node confirmed retrieved), ALND can be safely omitted. The FALSE trial demonstrated FNR of 4.2% with TAD, which is within the acceptable threshold.

wrong.A: Pre-NAC SLNB would stage the baseline axilla but the result would not determine the need for ALND — it is the post-NAC axillary status that guides management.
wrong.B: ALND is not mandatory after NAC in cN+ patients achieving ypN0 with TAD — this is the key change in paradigm from ACOSOG Z1071, SENTINA, and FALSE.
wrong.D: Breast pCR (ypT0) does not reliably predict axillary pCR (ypN0) — approximately 30–40% of patients with pCR in the breast have residual nodal disease. Axillary assessment is mandatory regardless of breast response.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Breast v5.2025 — cN+ post-NAC: TAD recommended; ypN0 with TAD → ALND omission acceptable
explanation.trial: FALSE Trial (Simons, JAMA Oncol 2022): TAD in cN1-N2 post-NAC — FNR 4.2%; supports ALND omission in ypN0 with adequate TAD sampling.
explanation.pearl: Post-NAC axillary algorithm for cN+ patients: Clip the positive node at diagnosis → post-NAC SLNB + TAD (retrieve clipped node) → if ypN0: omit ALND + give RNI → if ypN1+: ALND required.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with a 1.8 mm Breslow, non-ulcerated melanoma on the back, confirmed on narrow-margin excision biopsy (T2a). What is the recommended management for the primary site and nodal staging?

options.A: Wide excision with 1 cm margin only — SLNB is not indicated for T2 melanoma
options.B: Wide excision with 2 cm margin + sentinel lymph node biopsy
options.C: Wide excision with 3 cm margin — tumours >1 mm require 3 cm margins
options.D: Wide excision with 2 cm margin alone — nodal staging can be deferred until clinical nodal recurrence

explanation.correct: For T2 melanoma (1.01–2.0 mm Breslow), NCCN 2025 recommends wide excision with 1–2 cm margins (2 cm preferred when anatomically feasible). At 1.8 mm Breslow, 2 cm margin achieves equivalent locoregional control to 3 cm and is the standard. SLNB is recommended for all T2-T4 melanomas (SLN positivity rate ~15–20% for T2). MSLT-I showed that SLNB staging with early CLND in positive patients improves melanoma-specific survival compared to delayed detection at clinical relapse.

wrong.A: SLNB is recommended — not optional — for T2 melanoma. SLN positivity rate at 1.8 mm is ~15-20%, providing critical staging information and access to adjuvant therapy.
wrong.C: 3 cm margins are NOT superior to 2 cm for T3-T4 melanoma (MelMarT, UK Melanoma Study Group trials). For T2 melanoma, 1–2 cm is the evidence-based range.
wrong.D: Deferring nodal staging to clinical relapse was the historical approach. MSLT-I demonstrated that waiting for clinical nodal relapse results in significantly worse melanoma-specific survival compared to early SLNB staging and CLND.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Melanoma v2.2025 — T2 melanoma: 1–2 cm margin + SLNB recommended
explanation.trial: MSLT-I (Morton, NEJM 2006): early SLNB + CLND improved 10-yr melanoma-specific survival vs delayed CLND at clinical relapse for intermediate-thickness melanomas.
explanation.pearl: Melanoma margin algorithm: In situ → 0.5-1 cm; T1 (≤1 mm) → 1 cm; T2 (1-2 mm) → 1-2 cm; T3-T4 (>2 mm) → 2 cm. Deep margin should include deep fascia. 3 cm is never evidence-based.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman has a 3.2 mm Breslow, ulcerated melanoma (T3b) on the calf. Wide excision + SLNB is performed. The sentinel node shows a 1.2 mm micrometastasis (N1a, Stage IIIA). MSLT-II is now published. What is the most appropriate management of the axilla/regional basin?

options.A: Immediate completion lymph node dissection (CLND) of the popliteal and inguinal nodes — all positive SLNB patients require CLND
options.B: Ultrasound surveillance of the regional nodal basin every 3–4 months — CLND does not improve melanoma-specific survival (MSLT-II)
options.C: PET-CT scan — completion dissection is only performed if PET-CT confirms additional nodal involvement
options.D: External beam radiotherapy to the inguinal nodes only — CLND is not appropriate for the lower extremity

explanation.correct: MSLT-II (Faries, NEJM 2017) randomised 1934 SLNB-positive patients to CLND vs ultrasound surveillance. Melanoma-specific survival at 3 years was identical (86% vs 86%). CLND improved regional disease-free survival but significantly increased lymphedema risk (24% vs 6%). Current standard (NCCN 2025, ESMO 2023): after positive SLNB with micrometastatic disease, ultrasound surveillance of the regional basin is the preferred approach. CLND is reserved for macroscopic extracapsular extension, clinically apparent nodes, or patient preference after counselling.

wrong.A: This was the standard before MSLT-II but has been superseded. Routine CLND does not improve survival and significantly increases lymphedema.
wrong.C: PET-CT is used for staging systemic disease but does not determine the indication for CLND — the decision is based on MSLT-II evidence and clinical factors.
wrong.D: Radiotherapy can reduce regional recurrence after CLND in high-risk cases but does not substitute for the decision about CLND vs surveillance.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Melanoma v2.2025 / ESMO Melanoma 2023 — positive SLNB: CLND not routinely recommended; ultrasound surveillance preferred; adjuvant systemic therapy for Stage III
explanation.trial: MSLT-II (Faries, NEJM 2017): n=1934 SLNB+ patients; CLND vs surveillance — MSS identical; CLND increased lymphedema 24% vs 6%; DeCOG-SLT (Leiter, Lancet Oncol 2016): similar findings.
explanation.pearl: MSLT-II paradigm shift: positive SLNB → NO routine CLND. Start adjuvant systemic therapy (pembrolizumab or dabrafenib+trametinib if BRAF+) + ultrasound every 3 months. CLND only for macroscopic extracapsular extension or clinically palpable nodes.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man has Stage IIIC (N3a) melanoma with 5 positive inguinal nodes after wide excision and CLND. His tumour harbours a BRAF V600E mutation. Which adjuvant systemic therapy is most appropriate?

options.A: Adjuvant ipilimumab 10 mg/kg — the original adjuvant standard
options.B: Adjuvant dabrafenib + trametinib — BRAF/MEK combination for BRAF-mutated Stage III
options.C: Adjuvant chemotherapy with dacarbazine — the pre-immunotherapy standard
options.D: Observation only — adjuvant therapy is not indicated for surgically resected Stage III melanoma

explanation.correct: For resected Stage III BRAF V600-mutated melanoma, adjuvant dabrafenib + trametinib for 12 months is a standard of care based on COMBI-AD (Long, NEJM 2017): 5-year RFS 52% vs 36%; 5-year OS 55% vs 47%, significantly better than placebo. Pembrolizumab is an alternative (KEYNOTE-054), and nivolumab (CheckMate 238) are also options. For BRAF-mutated patients, both BRAF+MEK targeted therapy AND anti-PD-1 immunotherapy are evidence-based. The choice depends on toxicity profile, patient preference, and comorbidities.

wrong.A: Adjuvant ipilimumab 10 mg/kg showed DFS benefit (EORTC 18071) but has been largely superseded by anti-PD-1 agents (lower toxicity, higher efficacy) and BRAF+MEK therapy. Ipilimumab 10 mg/kg has grade 3/4 toxicity in >40% of patients.
wrong.C: Dacarbazine has no role in the adjuvant setting for melanoma — it was studied and abandoned before the immunotherapy era.
wrong.D: Adjuvant therapy is definitively indicated for resected Stage III melanoma — multiple trials show significant RFS and OS benefit.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Melanoma v2.2025 — resected Stage III BRAF+: adjuvant dabrafenib+trametinib OR pembrolizumab OR nivolumab for 12 months
explanation.trial: COMBI-AD (Long, NEJM 2017): dabrafenib+trametinib vs placebo for BRAF+ Stage III — 5yr RFS 52% vs 36%, 5yr OS 55% vs 47%; KEYNOTE-054 (Eggermont, NEJM 2018): pembrolizumab vs placebo — 3yr RFS 63.7% vs 44.1%.
explanation.pearl: Stage III adjuvant melanoma therapy: BRAF+ → choice of dabrafenib+trametinib OR pembrolizumab (12 months each). BRAF wild-type → pembrolizumab or nivolumab. Do NOT give BRAF inhibitor to BRAF wild-type — it is paradoxically tumour-promoting via paradoxical MAPK activation.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man is found to have a 4.5 mm Breslow, ulcerated melanoma (T4b) on the forearm, with 3 positive inguinal sentinel nodes (N2a). BRAF V600E mutation detected. He undergoes complete wide excision + CLND. Now Stage IIIC. He is fit for systemic therapy. What is the recommended first-line adjuvant option?

options.A: Nivolumab monotherapy — most favourable toxicity profile
options.B: Observation — Stage IIIC is too advanced for adjuvant therapy to be beneficial
options.C: Dabrafenib + trametinib or pembrolizumab — both are evidence-based adjuvant options for BRAF+ Stage IIIC
options.D: Ipilimumab + nivolumab — combination immunotherapy for high-risk Stage IIIC

explanation.correct: For BRAF V600-mutated resected Stage III melanoma, both dabrafenib+trametinib (COMBI-AD) and pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-054) are evidence-based adjuvant options — either is appropriate and NCCN-endorsed. Nivolumab (CheckMate 238) is also a valid option. The combination ipilimumab+nivolumab is NOT approved in the adjuvant resected setting (it is approved for unresectable/metastatic disease). The choice between targeted therapy and immunotherapy in the adjuvant BRAF+ setting is individualised.

wrong.A: Nivolumab monotherapy is an option (CheckMate 238) but not necessarily 'most favourable' over pembrolizumab — both anti-PD-1 agents have similar safety profiles in the adjuvant setting.
wrong.B: Stage IIIC benefits significantly from adjuvant therapy — COMBI-AD and KEYNOTE-054 included Stage IIIC patients with large RFS and OS benefits.
wrong.D: Nivolumab + ipilimumab combination is not approved in the adjuvant setting for resected melanoma — it carries unacceptably high grade 3/4 toxicity (>50%) without established superiority over monotherapy in the adjuvant context.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Melanoma v2.2025 — Stage III BRAF+: adjuvant dabrafenib+trametinib or pembrolizumab or nivolumab × 12 months
explanation.trial: COMBI-AD (Long, NEJM 2017) — Stage III BRAF+ adjuvant; KEYNOTE-054 (Eggermont, NEJM 2018) — Stage III all-comers adjuvant pembrolizumab.
explanation.pearl: Stage IIIC is NOT too advanced for adjuvant therapy — it has the greatest absolute benefit from treatment intensification. Do not withhold adjuvant therapy in fit IIIC patients.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman is found to have a Stage IV melanoma with lung-only metastases (M1b), BRAF wild-type. She is PS0 and fit for systemic therapy. Which is the most appropriate first-line systemic treatment?

options.A: Dacarbazine chemotherapy — the historical standard for Stage IV melanoma
options.C: Vemurafenib monotherapy — BRAF inhibitor for all Stage IV melanoma
options.D: High-dose interleukin-2 (IL-2) — the original immunotherapy for Stage IV

explanation.correct: For BRAF wild-type Stage IV melanoma, checkpoint immunotherapy is the only effective systemic option — BRAF inhibitors do NOT work in BRAF wild-type tumours (and are paradoxically tumour-promoting). Nivolumab + ipilimumab is the preferred first-line regimen based on CheckMate 067 (7-year OS 49% vs 21% for ipilimumab alone; median OS 72 months). Nivolumab or pembrolizumab monotherapy are alternatives with better tolerability. Dacarbazine is historically used but has <15% response rate and no OS benefit — now abandoned. IL-2 is toxic and largely superseded.

wrong.A: Dacarbazine is the pre-immunotherapy standard with <15% ORR and no OS benefit — it has been abandoned as first-line therapy in fit patients.
wrong.C: Vemurafenib is a BRAF V600 inhibitor — it has NO activity in BRAF wild-type melanoma and may paradoxically accelerate tumour growth through MAPK pathway bypass.
wrong.D: High-dose IL-2 had 5-8% durable complete response rate historically but has extreme toxicity (pulmonary edema, hypotension) and has been superseded by anti-PD-1 ± anti-CTLA-4 combinations.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Melanoma v2.2025 — Stage IV BRAF wild-type: first-line nivolumab+ipilimumab or pembrolizumab or nivolumab
explanation.trial: CheckMate 067 (Wolchok, NEJM 2022): 7-yr OS nivo+ipi 49% vs nivo 44% vs ipi 21%; median OS 72.1 months (nivo+ipi) — established combination as standard.
explanation.pearl: BRAF wild-type Stage IV → immunotherapy only. Critically: never give BRAF inhibitor to BRAF wild-type melanoma. Confirm BRAF status (tissue or ctDNA) before any targeted therapy. PD-L1 high + BRAF wild-type: nivolumab monotherapy vs combination both acceptable.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 0.65 mm, non-ulcerated melanoma (T1a) on her shoulder, confirmed on narrow-margin excision biopsy. What is the recommended wide excision margin and sentinel node biopsy approach?

options.A: 1 cm margin + SLNB — all melanomas require SLNB regardless of thickness
options.B: 1 cm margin alone — SLNB is not recommended for T1a melanoma
options.C: 2 cm margin + SLNB — standard for all invasive melanomas
options.D: 0.5 cm margin — T1a melanoma is low-risk and requires minimal margins

explanation.correct: For T1a melanoma (<0.8 mm, no ulceration, no adverse features), NCCN 2025 recommends 1 cm wide excision margin. SLNB is NOT recommended because the SLN positivity rate for T1a is <5%, making the procedure low-yield, and there is no survival benefit demonstrated for SLNB in this group. The WHO Melanoma Trial 1 and subsequent studies confirmed 1 cm margin equivalence to wider resection for thin melanomas.

wrong.A: SLNB is not recommended for T1a — SLN positivity rate is <5% and no survival benefit has been shown. Routine SLNB in T1a is not cost-effective.
wrong.C: 2 cm margin is recommended for T3-T4 (>2 mm Breslow), not T1a. Wider margins increase morbidity without oncological benefit for thin tumours.
wrong.D: 0.5 cm is the margin for melanoma in situ, not for invasive T1a melanoma. All invasive melanomas require at least 1 cm.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Melanoma v2.2025 — T1a: 1 cm margin; SLNB not recommended; discuss for T1b (0.8-1.0mm or ulcerated)
explanation.trial: WHO Melanoma Trial 1 (Veronesi, NEJM 1988): 1 cm vs 3 cm margin for thin melanomas — equivalent OS and local recurrence; 1 cm is the established standard.
explanation.pearl: T1a (<0.8mm, no ulceration) = 1 cm margin, no SLNB. T1b (ulceration OR 0.8-1.0mm) = 1 cm margin, discuss SLNB (SLN+ rate 5-12%). T2+ = 1-2 cm margin, SLNB recommended.
